studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention programme lasts 8 weeks (8 modules). Primary outcome is assessed immediately after training and again 3 months later. The study ran from July 2014 until May 2015 (completion date 26/05/2015).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (IG) received access to the online ELEVIDA fatigue management programme; Control group (CG) was a wait-list control group that received the programme after the study completed. Total target enrollment: 204.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention Group): Participants received a personal access code to the ELEVIDA online CBT-based fatigue training tool for 8 weeks. Arm 2 (Wait-list Control Group): Participants were informed about their option to enter the FATIMA programme after the study and received a brief explanation about the need for control groups in RCTs.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Fatigue measured by the Chalder Fatigue Scale (Cella & Chalder et al., 2010) assessed pre- and post-training immediately after and 3 months later. Secondary outcomes: 1. FSMC (Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions, Penner et al., 2009); 2. Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS, Zigmond & Snaith, 1983); 3. Hamburg Quality of Life Questionnaire in MS (HAQUAMS, Gold et al., 2001); 4. Multiple Sclerosis Neuropsychological Screening Questionnaire (MSNQ, Benedict et al., 2003). Exploratory: 5. Cognitive and Behavioural Responses to Symptoms Questionnaire (Skerett & Moss-Morris, 2006); 6. Brief Illness Perception Questionnaire (Broadbent et al., 2006); 7. Coping Self Efficacy Scale (CSES, Chesney et al., 2006); 8. Frenchay Activity Index (Turnbull et al., 2000); 9. Revised NEO Personality Inventory (Costa & McCrae, 1992). A subgroup also underwent clinical examination and MRI (DTI and resting state fMRI connectivity measures). Screening used HAQUAMS fatigue subscale (score ≥2) and FSMC total fatigue score ≥43 for inclusion.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at week 24 (12 weeks after primary endpoint)
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasted 12 weeks; outcomes were assessed at baseline, week 12 (post-intervention/primary endpoint), and week 24 (follow-up).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 139 (ELEVIDA online intervention) and C = 136 (waitlist control group)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: 12-week self-guided interactive online fatigue management programme (ELEVIDA) based on CBT and related psychotherapeutic approaches (e.g., mindfulness), n=139, 82% female, mean age 40.8, median PDDS 3.0. Control arm: waitlist control group, n=136, 79% female, mean age 41.9, median PDDS 3.0.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Chalder Fatigue Scale (assessed at baseline, week 12, and week 24). Secondary outcomes not explicitly listed in the abstract. Patient Determined Disease Steps (PDDS) used as a measure of disability at baseline. Randomisation was 1:1 with no blocking or stratification.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12 (primary endpoint), the ELEVIDA group showed significantly greater reductions in Chalder Fatigue Scale scores compared to control (intention-to-treat: between-group mean difference 2.74 points; 95% CI 1.16 to 4.32; p=0.0007; effect size d=0.53). Effects were sustained at week 24 (intention-to-treat: between-group mean difference 2.19 points; 95% CI 0.57 to 3.82; p=0.0080). The trial provides evidence for the effectiveness of a self-guided internet-based intervention to reduce fatigue in MS.

audit.study_title: Online therapy programme for fatigue in multiple sclerosis
audit.note: registry-only: outcome measures captured under collected_data; no reported results

audit.study_title: Randomised controlled trial of a self-guided online fatigue intervention in multiple sclerosis.
